stories_covered: Apple rebands Siri on Google Gemini
Uber cuts 23% of HR, expands autonomous driving
harry_takes: SpaceX IPO pricing mechanism is riskier than normal bank-led discovery because Elon fixed the price in advance, removing information asymmetry that normally pops IPOs
Apple pragmatically gave up on competing with their own AI model and instead partnered with Google—the smart move because they control handsets and consumer experience, not enterprise scale
Founders need to stop making excuses about needing fat teams; lean startups like Lovable with 146 people hitting $500M ARR prove that bloat is a choice, not a necessity
The upcoming market environment will raise the bar for everyone; LPs now expect 7-8x returns routinely, which cascades down to pressure on founders and forces tighter unit economics
harry_boldest_quote: I'm kind of contemptuous of startups that need to be fat. I'm like, what's your excuse?
predictions: Lemkin: SpaceX IPO will likely be a dud on day one and won't trade up dramatically; expects the stock to underperform the IPO price within 12 months as valuation reverts (base rate on 70x forward sales IPOs show dips)
Lemkin: Over the next year, fundamental value will reassert itself and SpaceX stock price dips despite being an amazing company
Harry: Startups will become roughly half the size they used to be for equivalent revenue; lean teams with AI leverage will become the norm, not the exception
Lemkin: AI-efficient businesses with small headcounts will see better employee economics and will be more attractive places to work than bloated enterprises
disagreements: Lemkin predicts SpaceX IPO will be a dud and underperform; Harry counters that day-one direction is unknowable but agrees medium-term valuation reverts. Lemkin expects enterprise businesses will still need large sales forces; Harry disputes this, arguing founders are choosing lean go-to-market to avoid bloat culture.
harry_role: Harry moderates as host but aggressively debates both panelists as an equal. He challenges Lemkin's IPO dip prediction, pushes back on enterprise sales force inevitability, and drives the anti-bloat thesis. He defers on technical IPO mechanics to Lemkin's expertise but owns the strategic framing.
notable: The episode was recorded Tuesday, June 11, with SpaceX IPO roadshow happening Thursday—creating real-time tension as their predictions were about to be tested within 48 hours.
